audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Exodus / Shemot text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Israelite multiplication, Pharaoh's oppression, forced labor, midwives' resistance, and birth politics.

verses.restored: And these are the names of the sons of Israel who came into Egypt with Jacob; each came with his household:
Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah,
Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin,
Dan and Naphtali, Gad and Asher.
All the souls/lives going out from Jacob's thigh were seventy souls/lives, and Joseph was in Egypt.
And Joseph died, and all his brothers, and all that generation.
But the children of Israel were fruitful, swarmed, multiplied, and became exceedingly mighty; and the land was filled with them.
And a new king arose over Egypt who did not know Joseph.
He said to his people, Look, the people of the children of Israel are more numerous and mighty than we.
Come, let us deal shrewdly with them, lest they multiply, and it happen that when war is called, they also join our enemies and fight against us and go up from the land.
So they set over them forced-labor chiefs to afflict them with burdens; and they built storage cities for Pharaoh: Pithom and Raamses.
But the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and spread out; and they felt dread before the children of Israel.
Egypt made the children of Israel serve with crushing labor.
They made their lives bitter with hard service in mortar and brick and in every kind of service in the field; all their service with which they made them serve was with crushing force.
The king of Egypt spoke to the Hebrew midwives, one whose name was Shiphrah and the second whose name was Puah,
and he said, When you serve as midwives to the Hebrew women and see upon the birth-stools, if it is a son, put him to death; but if it is a daughter, she shall live.
But the midwives feared/revered the Divine, and they did not do as king of Egypt spoke to them; they let the boys live.
The king of Egypt called the midwives and said to them, Why have you done this thing and let the boys live?
The midwives said to Pharaoh, Because the Hebrew women are not like the Egyptian women; for they are vigorous/life-filled, and before the midwife comes to them, they have given birth.
And the Divine did good to the midwives, and the people multiplied and became very mighty.
And because the midwives feared/revered the Divine, he made houses/households for them.
Then Pharaoh commanded all his people, saying, Every son born, cast him into the Nile, but every daughter let live.

translation_notes: Hebrew title Shemot means Names; Exodus opens with names against imperial erasure.
Multiplication language echoes Genesis creation blessing.
Avad/avodah service language is central: Egypt turns service into crushing bondage, while Exodus will move toward service/worship of YHWH.
Midwives Shiphrah and Puah are named resistance figures; their reverence for Divine overrides Pharaoh's death-command.
safety_note: The chapter critiques Pharaoh's imperial oppression, forced labor, and birth violence. Do not turn Egypt/Egyptians into an ethnic enemy; the target is oppressive state power and fear-based domination.
